content:
In the world of executive protection, the practice of counter-surveillance has emerged as an incredibly effective yet underutilized strategy. Despite its recognition among professionals, the true potential of counter-surveillance remains largely untapped in executive protection operations, a fact that has been highlighted by recent tragic incidents in Mexico City.

Mexico City, like many other urban centers around the world, has experienced a series of high-profile attacks in recent years, highlighting the importance of counter-surveillance in protecting individuals and preventing such incidents. These attacks have targeted prominent figures, including politicians, religious leaders, security officials, and business owners. The consequences of these attacks have been devastating, not only for the victims and their families but also for the sense of security within the city. These incidents include the 2018 home invasion of Norberto Rivera, former Primate Archbishop of Mexico, the 2020 attack on Mexico City police chief Omar García Harfuch, the 2021 attack on restaurant owner Eduardo Beaven, and the 2022 attacks on Congresswoman Gabriela Sanches and renowned journalist Ciro Gómez Leyva.

These incidents have revealed a troubling pattern. Subsequent investigations have shown that the victims had been subjected to hostile surveillance by criminals for prolonged periods prior to the attacks. In some cases, this surveillance went unnoticed for many months, allowing criminals to gather valuable information, plan their actions, and strike when the target was most vulnerable. The lack of timely detection and intervention during the surveillance phase has been a critical factor in the success of these criminal operations.

Counter-surveillance, when effectively implemented, has the potential to disrupt this initial observation stage and significantly reduce the risk and uncertainty associated with

reactive measures. By proactively identifying and countering surveillance activities, security professionals can thwart criminal plans, prevent attacks, and save lives. However, despite its proven effectiveness, counter-surveillance remains an underutilized strategy in the field of executive protection.

One of the reasons why counter-surveillance is underutilized may be the misconception that criminals are primarily deterred by the presence of armed agents or visible security measures. Surprisingly, that is not true.

To better understand the logic behind this peculiar stance, it is imperative to first understand the mechanisms of counter-surveillance and why it is so effective. Every assault follows a preparatory cycle.

This cycle consists of several stages, but we will focus on the most significant ones:

Hostile Intelligence:

This phase involves criminals gathering pertinent information about their potential victim, which they can then exploit to select them as a target for robbery, kidnapping, or any form of aggression against a premeditated target.

Once the victim is selected and the necessary amount of information is gathered, the most complex, laborious, and dangerous aspect of attack preparation begins: surveillance of the victim. Although criminals can track their victim electronically, using methods such as following the location of their

phone through cell towers, installing GPS devices on the victim's vehicles, or even placing cameras in common areas of their offices or residences, nothing can substitute physical and in-person surveillance of the individual in various locations and circumstances. It is in these contexts where their behaviors, the presence of a security team (if applicable), general movements within specific areas of interest at specific times, escape routes, and dynamics are outlined.

During this operational phase, criminals become highly vulnerable to detection. Even if they manage to evade arrest at the moment of the attack (which is unfortunately frequent), any mistake made during this stage can lead to their subsequent capture, a notion that remains at the forefront of their minds. For this reason, it can be said that high-profile criminals fear countersurveillance more than weapons.

We had the opportunity to speak with Olivera Cirkovic, former leader of one of the Pink Panthers jewelry thief cells. This notorious gang is known for its meticulously planned robberies of jewelry stores not only in Europe, but even in Dubai and Japan. The cumulative sum stolen by various cells within this organization is estimated at half a billion euros.

Olivera Ćirković was a professional basketball player, one of the most successful and recognized of her time in the former Yugoslavia. After concluding her basketball career, she was even the sports director of the famous Serbian team, Red Star Belgrade. However, after retiring from sports, Olivera ventured into a life of crime, leading one of the Pink Panthers cells. She was arrested in March 2012 but made history a few months later by becoming the only woman to successfully escape from a high-security prison in Greece. Surprisingly, her escape received significant public support. During her robberies, no one was injured or killed, earning her nicknames such as "Amazon Woman," "Dragon Woman," "Queen," and others. In September of the same year, she was arrested again and sentenced to thirty-two and a half years in prison. After numerous appeals and curiously enjoying significant public support due to her sports career, physical beauty, and "celebrity" status, she was released in August of

2017. Currently, she is a painter, writer, and provides crime mitigation consultancy to security agencies.

During our conversation, she mentioned that she carried out hundreds of attacks worldwide, some of which involved kidnapping jewelers to gain access to jewelry stores for the purpose of robbery. She told me that in most of these robberies, the security personnel were armed, but this never deterred her. She stated that weapons only scared common criminals, not her or her team. She relied on prolonged and detailed observation of the victim, attacking unexpectedly during moments of maximum vulnerability and distraction. As a result, in all these attacks, the protectors were unable to draw their weapons or react in any way.

During her last robbery, she kidnapped a jeweler in Athens. This was preceded by extensive observation of the victim, during which she and her team changed vehicles three times along a single route to minimize exposure. For the same reasons, she avoided following the victim on one-way streets and instead resumed surveillance on main avenues. She even installed surveillance cameras in the building's parking lot to better analyze the victim's movements and habits. As a result, the actual attack unfolded quickly and efficiently.

She was confident in her meticulous preparation. However, her greatest fear was being identified during the observation of the victim, as that could lead to her arrest before or even after the crime.

At the moment of the attack, she felt no fear because the victim had been thoroughly studied. The attackers used stolen vehicles, wore balaclavas, and relied on the element of surprise. Everything happened in an instant, giving them the advantage.

However, her fear arose during the surveillance of the victim. Throughout this prolonged process, she could not move in stolen cars or use disguises that would attract attention. She had to remain fully identifiable at all times, which terrified her. Her fear was so intense that if anyone approached her during this process, whether a passerby or a security guard, and asked what she was doing there or showed interest in her, her group would immediately abandon that particular victim.

She always said that her worst fear was being observed by someone without realizing it. Ironically, something like that eventually led to her arrest.

While observing the jewelry store we are referring to, Olivera was a fugitive from a Greek prison, so she had to minimize her exposure. She waited in the vehicle while instructing a gang member to go to the jewelry store and observe certain pieces and other circumstances inside the building. To leave no traces, the criminal wore gloves. Once the task was completed, the criminal left the jewelry store and, while passing an Athenian cafe, took off the gloves and threw them away. This was noticed by the cafe owner, who found it suspicious that someone was wearing gloves in the Athens heat in August, and decided to follow him. The criminal got into the vehicle where Olivera was waiting and they drove off, while the cafe owner noted the vehicle's license plate and reported this suspicious incident to the

police. The police recorded the call but took no immediate action.

A month later, the Pink Panthers kidnapped the jeweler and robbed the store. At this point, the Athens police requested reports of all suspicious events reported around the jewelry store in recent months, and the jeweler's call appeared. The police investigated the license plates of the reported vehicle and suspected it was the one rented by the Pink Panthers. They waited for the criminals to return the vehicle, followed them, and made the arrest.

In this way, Olivera's fear of being observed without realizing it became reality. This was, in fact, circumstantial countersurveillance carried out by a well-meaning citizen. However, if a specialized countersurveillance team had been available, they could have acted preemptively and prevented the kidnapping.

If we add to this the examples of attacks and kidnappings in Mexico, where it was eventually demonstrated through the use of cameras that the victims had been under surveillance for several months, it becomes clear that countersurveillance is the most effective protective measure to prevent such attacks. It is a measure that criminals fear and, paradoxically, is underutilized in executive protection. The fact that surveillance in these attacks was detected using cameras after some time indicates a lack of sophistication on the part of the criminals in carrying out professional surveillance. At the same time, it demonstrates that the protectors lacked the knowledge to even detect such poorly executed surveillance.

Simply reviewing the cameras periodically would have been enough to detect the criminals before, rather than after, the event.

Here is another clear example of very poorly executed surveillance. On July 23 of last year in Ecuador, the popular mayor of the city of Manta, Agustín Intriago, was assassinated during a public event in the 15 de Septiembre neighborhood in Manta, Manabí province. Weeks later, the Ecuadorian Police revealed that the victim had been followed for several days by a blue pickup truck disguised as a water vendor, carrying large containers. This vehicle followed the mayor everywhere, until the last day when he was assassinated, constantly providing information and guiding the killer. It was, in fact, poorly executed hostile surveillance, but unfortunately, it was enough because the protectors lacked the basic knowledge to detect it.

One of the earliest and most significant high-profile kidnappings that occurred in Mexico during the 1990s triggered a wave of executive protection in the country. This particular incident involved the kidnapping of one of the most prominent bankers of that era. Surprisingly, this crime was preceded by a staggering 14 months of hostile surveillance.

Curiously, the surveillance was easily detectable, as it was initiated by a flower vendor who discarded the flowers after the victim left his house and began his daily route to work. This constant pattern persisted for 14 months, making it easily identifiable if a countersurveillance team had existed, which, unfortunately, was not the case.

Subsequent investigations into kidnappings, assassinations, etc., reveal that in Executive Protection, we have adopted the wrong approach. We continue to insist on close protection, the use of weapons, vehicles, hand-to-hand combat, and formations, while the most important and effective aspect, which is surveillance detection and countersurveillance, is rarely mentioned or discussed in conferences, courses, and specialized literature.

So, the question that arises is: What else has to happen before we set aside the dangerous fantasies surrounding weapons and reactive measures, and shift our focus toward executive protection through proactive methods of countersurveillance and early warnings? By adopting countersurveillance as a core strategy in executive protection, we can avoid the unnecessary loss of lives of both the protected and their protectors.

Furthermore, technological advances in surveillance systems, including facial recognition, advanced analytics, and real-time monitoring, provide security professionals with powerful tools to effectively detect and respond to surveillance activities. These technologies, when integrated into a comprehensive security strategy, can strengthen overall protection measures and allow for early intervention in potential threats.

This change of focus is essential to staying ahead of criminals. It is time to unlock the true potential of countersurveillance and master the art of proactive protection.
